title: Onion bhaji
subtitle: Crisp spiced onion fritters
excerpt: Thinly sliced onion bound in a spiced chickpea flour batter and fried until crisp and golden. The best starter from the British-Indian restaurant repertoire and very easy to make at home. Makes about 12 fritters.
sourceNotes: Onion bhaji (also pakora or bhajia) is the British-Indian restaurant version of a dish found across India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh. Documented by Pat Chapman in The Curry Club Indian Restaurant Cookbook (1984) and by Madhur Jaffrey in Invitation to Indian Cooking (1973). The chickpea flour (gram flour) batter is the consistent element across regional versions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter can be mixed up to 30 minutes ahead. Rest with the onion for that time — the onion releases water which gives the batter better consistency.

content.text: The batter should be thick enough to cling to the onion but not so thick that it forms a doughy shell. After mixing, leave it to rest for 10 minutes — the onion releases water, which loosens the batter naturally and means you rarely need to add extra water. The ideal consistency is like a thick porridge that drops heavily off a spoon.

items.prepNote: or one small green chilli, finely chopped
finely chopped — mix into the batter or scatter to serve

content.text: Serve with plain yoghurt stirred with a little ground cumin and salt, or with mango chutney. The bhajis are best eaten within 5 minutes of frying while still completely crisp.

Put {{onion}} thinly sliced onions in a large bowl. Add {{chickpea-flour}} of chickpea flour, {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander, {{turmeric}} of ground turmeric, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, {{garam-masala}} of garam masala, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Mix well with your hands, squeezing the onion into the flour as you go. Leave to rest for 10 minutes — the onion will release liquid that loosens the batter. If the mixture still seems too dry to hold together, add a tablespoon of cold water at a time until you reach a thick, dropping consistency. Heat {{sunflower-oil}} of sunflower oil in a deep saucepan to 175°C. Fry heaped tablespoons of the mixture in batches of 4, for 3–4 minutes per batch until golden and crisp. Do not crowd the pan. Drain on kitchen paper and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Bhajis falling apart in the oil
items.cause: Batter too wet and loose, or oil not hot enough so they spread before setting.
items.fix: The batter should hold together when squeezed. Test the oil with a small piece of batter — it should sizzle immediately and float. If it sinks, the oil is not ready.

items.symptom: Pale and soft rather than golden and crisp
items.cause: Oil temperature too low, or too many bhajis in the pan cooling the oil.
items.fix: Fry in small batches of four maximum. Between batches, let the oil return to temperature.

items.cause: Bhajis too large, or fried too fast at too high a temperature so the outside browns before the inside cooks.
items.fix: Keep them small — a heaped tablespoon per bhaji. 175°C is the right temperature: hot enough to colour quickly, not so hot that the outside burns.

content.text: Onion bhaji is the British-Indian restaurant starter, ordered so reflexively that most people barely notice it on the menu — it simply arrives. The version served in restaurants is typically rounder and denser than the thin, lacy pakoras found across India, a form that developed from the practical constraints of restaurant cooking. The version here is closer to the restaurant style but made at home, where you can control the temperature and eat them immediately rather than having them sit in a warming drawer. The chickpea flour batter is naturally gluten-free and vegan, which makes this one of the few deep-fried dishes that a table with multiple dietary requirements can usually share.
